content:
Unfortunately, Understanding Is Still Full Price

10 min read
Just now

--

AI can now generate more code in an afternoon than a developer once wrote in a week. That sounds like progress — until someone has to understand, review, secure, and maintain it. Linus Torvalds has quietly exposed the real shift: in the age of artificial intelligence, writing code is becoming cheap. Knowing what should be written, why it should exist, and when to delete it is becoming priceless.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Linus Torvalds is no longer impressed by who typed the code. Perhaps the rest of us should catch up.
The popular image of a great programmer was reassuringly cinematic. A dark room. Three monitors. An alarming quantity of caffeine. Fingers moving across a keyboard at a speed normally associated with concert pianists and people attempting to cancel an airline subscription. The programmer typed.
💥 Master Any Skills in 3 Months
📚 Up to 50% OFF Premium Courses

Press enter or click to view image in full size

That was the job. Or at least that was the visible part of the job, the part that looked impressive in films, recruitment videos, and photographs accompanying articles titled Ten Habits of Highly Effective Developers Who Wake Up at 4:30 a.m. Then Linus Torvalds, the creator of Linux and Git, casually wandered onto a stage in Mumbai and complicated the entire mythology. He explained that he hardly reads code anymore.
Instead, he reads pull-request descriptions. He tries to understand what contributors want to achieve. When he notices a problem, he may suggest a patch, but the people who actually know the relevant subsystem are expected to produce the final solution. In other words, one of the most famous programmers alive now spends much of his time understanding intentions, evaluating decisions, and coordinating other people’s expertise. This sounds almost scandalous.
It is a little like Michelangelo announcing that he no longer touches marble and now mostly asks whether the statue really needs another elbow. But Torvalds has not stopped engineering. He has simply reached the level at which engineering is no longer confused with typing. And that distinction may be the most important lesson of the AI coding revolution.
We Mistook the Keyboard for the Profession
Programming has always had an identity problem. We describe the work using the most mechanical part of it. We “write code,” just as authors write books and composers write music. The phrase suggests that the production of symbols is the main event. But code is not the product.
The product is a system that does something useful without accidentally transferring the company payroll to a teenager in Belarus. Code is merely the extremely detailed set of instructions through which we attempt to persuade a computer to behave. Sometimes the persuasion works. Sometimes the computer interprets our intentions with the literal-minded enthusiasm of a genie in a cautionary fairy tale.
AI has now become extraordinarily good at producing those instructions. Give it a feature description, and it can generate functions, classes, tests, documentation, configuration files, database queries, and seventeen enthusiastic comments explaining code that nobody requested. This is impressive. It is also dangerous, because it allows us to produce mistakes at industrial scale.
Before AI, writing 10,000 lines of questionable code required time, dedication, and at least some familiarity with the keyboard. Now it requires one paragraph and a subscription. We have automated the easy part while preserving every difficulty that made software engineering difficult in the first place.
The Great Code Inflation
Economists understand what happens when something becomes abundant: its individual value usually falls. Photography offers a useful analogy. When every photograph required film, chemicals, and a modest financial commitment, people took twelve pictures during an entire holiday. Today, we take twelve pictures while trying to decide whether the hotel breakfast egg is scrambled or legally classified as insulation material. The number of photographs exploded. The number of memorable photographs did not. AI is doing something similar to code.
We are entering an age of code inflation: more functions, more prototypes, more applications, more pull requests, more bug reports, more repositories, and more software apparently designed to help us manage all the software we generated to help us manage the original software. Code is becoming abundant. Attention is not. Understanding is not. Judgment is not.
And the number of experienced maintainers willing to spend Saturday afternoon determining why your AI-generated authentication layer stores passwords in a publicly accessible spreadsheet remains stubbornly limited. The scarce resource in software development is no longer the ability to produce code. It is the ability to decide which code deserves to exist.
AI Is the Most Productive Junior Developer in History
AI coding assistants are often described as copilots, agents, pair programmers, or digital colleagues. These metaphors are flattering. A more accurate description might be: An immensely knowledgeable junior developer who never sleeps, never feels embarrassed, and has read the entire internet without necessarily understanding why half of it was wrong.
This junior developer is remarkably fast. It can generate a working prototype before a human developer has finished choosing a name for the repository. It knows dozens of programming languages. It can explain obscure libraries, translate between frameworks, and write regular expressions that resemble inscriptions recovered from an alien spacecraft. It also has several charming personality traits. It wants to please you. It dislikes admitting uncertainty. And when it does not know the answer, it sometimes invents one with the serene confidence of a consultant presenting a PowerPoint slide titled “Strategic Synergy Roadmap.” The resulting code often looks convincing. That is precisely the problem.
Obviously, bad code is relatively safe. Someone sees it and says, “Good Lord, no.” Plausible code is more dangerous. It passes the visual inspection. It contains sensible variable names. It includes comments. It may even have tests — tests carefully designed to confirm that the code behaves exactly as the code currently behaves. The machine has not merely generated a mistake. It has generated a professionally dressed mistake with supporting documentation.
The Question Is No Longer “Does It Work?”
For small experiments, “Does it work?” may be enough. You ask an AI to create a tool that renames your holiday photos. The tool works. Excellent. Nobody needs to establish a governance committee. But production software lives much longer and more complicated lives. The relevant questions become: What happens when usage increases a hundredfold? What happens when one service becomes unavailable? What happens when the input is malicious? What happens when regulations change? What happens when the original developer leaves? What happens when the AI-generated dependency is abandoned by its maintainer, who turns out to be one exhausted person named Kevin? And, most importantly: Who understands this well enough to fix it at three in the morning?
AI can generate the code. It cannot be placed on call. It cannot take responsibility during the incident review. It cannot sit in a meeting while six executives ask why a “minor optimization” caused customers in three countries to receive emails addressed to “Dear Undefined.” Responsibility remains inconveniently human.
Torvalds has repeatedly described AI as a powerful tool while rejecting the idea that it changes the fundamentals of programming. He has also stressed that developers must understand the final result if they expect to maintain it over time. That is the quiet part nobody wants to put into an AI productivity presentation. Generation is fast. Comprehension is slow. Maintenance is slower. Accountability does not appear in the benchmark.
The Pull Request Is the New Crime Scene
In the pre-AI era, submitting a large pull request at least suggested that somebody had suffered while creating it. There was a certain moral balance. The author spent three days writing the code. The reviewer spent three hours asking why it existed. AI has broken this equilibrium.
A contributor can now generate a 2,000-line pull request during lunch and send it to a maintainer whose afternoon has thereby been converted into archaeological fieldwork. The author’s cost approaches zero. The reviewer’s cost does not.
This produces a peculiar new form of economic pollution: code that is cheap to create but expensive for everyone else to evaluate. Open-source maintainers are already encountering floods of AI-assisted bug reports and contributions whose review cost may exceed their value. Torvalds himself has described Linux security channels being overwhelmed by duplicate or poorly routed AI-generated findings. The internet has invented drive-by programming.
Someone arrives, asks an AI to “improve performance,” submits the resulting patch, and disappears into the digital night. The maintainer is left examining 700 changed lines and a pull-request description that says: Optimized code for better scalability and robustness. This sentence contains no information, but it does contain two of the software industry’s favourite nouns.
The Future Senior Engineer May Write Less Code
For years, programmers have been measured using wonderfully crude metrics. Lines of code. Number of commits. Tickets closed. Pull requests merged. These measures are attractive because they produce numbers, and numbers allow management to create charts. Once a chart exists, everyone feels that something scientific has occurred.
But in an AI-saturated environment, measuring productivity by code volume becomes absurd. The most dangerous developer may soon be the person producing the most code.
The most valuable developer may be the one who deletes 5,000 lines, rejects three unnecessary services, identifies an existing library, and prevents the organization from building a distributed architecture for a problem that could have been solved with a scheduled email. Writing code will remain important. But not writing code will become an increasingly valuable skill.
The senior engineer of the future will not necessarily be the person who can generate the largest system. It will be the person who knows where the system should stop.
Vibe Coding Is Fine — Until the Vibe Reaches Production
There is nothing inherently wrong with vibe coding. It is marvelous for experiments, prototypes, personal tools, educational projects, and that app you are absolutely certain will revolutionize the restaurant industry because it allows users to rate individual bread baskets.
The trouble begins when prototype psychology enters production. A prototype asks: Can this work? A production system asks: Can this continue working after the original team has forgotten why it was built? These are very different questions. AI excels at the first. Engineering exists because of the second.
Vibe coding treats software as an event: you describe what you want, the machine produces it, the screen lights up, and somewhere a venture capitalist whispers the word “disruption.” Real engineering treats software as a relationship.
The system must be understood, maintained, secured, updated, monitored, documented, and eventually retired with slightly more dignity than an abandoned WordPress plugin. The demo is the wedding. Maintenance is the marriage. AI is extremely good at weddings.
Taste Becomes a Technical Skill
When production becomes easy, selection becomes difficult. This has already happened in writing, photography, music, design, and online publishing. We can create more than ever before, yet the central problem remains
